title: Hearts, Hugs, and Chocolate Galore!
story: Red hearts cover store windows and chocolate boxes fill the shelves because February 14 is Valentine's Day! People celebrate love and friendship by giving cards, flowers, and sweet treats to the special people in their lives. Kids exchange colorful valentines at school parties, while grown-ups surprise each other with roses and romantic dinners. Some people wear red or pink clothes to show their Valentine's spirit. Social media fills up with heart emojis and love messages all day long. Whether it's a handmade card for mom or a box of chocolates for a crush, Valentine's Day is all about spreading kindness and showing people you care about them.
activity_question: What do people commonly give each other on Valentine's Day?
follow_up_question: Who would you want to give a special valentine to and why?

title: Going in Circles for Fun!
story: Every February 14, people celebrate National Ferris Wheel Day by riding these giant spinning wheels that lift you high into the sky! Amusement parks across the country offer special deals and discounts on Ferris wheel rides. Families pack into colorful gondolas and slowly rotate up, up, up to see amazing views of their city or park below. Some people propose marriage at the top, while others just enjoy the peaceful ride and take selfies with the scenery. Many parks decorate their Ferris wheels with extra lights and music for the day. It's the perfect way to see the world from a bird's-eye view!
activity_question: What do some people do at the top of a Ferris wheel on this holiday?
bonus_fact: The first Ferris wheel was built in 1893 and was 264 feet tall!

title: Cream-Filled Chocolate Heaven Day
story: February 14 isn't just Valentine's Day - it's also National Cream-Filled Chocolates Day! People celebrate by biting into their favorite cream-filled treats like chocolate truffles, bonbons, and candy-filled hearts. Chocolate shops display fancy boxes of assorted creams, from vanilla and strawberry to exotic flavors like lavender or salted caramel. Many families make it a tradition to share a box together, playing the guessing game of what flavor they'll get next. Social media fills with photos of people's chocolate hauls and messy cream-filled bites. It's the perfect excuse to indulge in these sweet, gooey treasures!

Count how many they can eat in one minute
activity_answer: Play a guessing game about the flavors

title: Shh! It's Library Lovers Day!
story: Every February 14, book fans celebrate Library Lovers Day by showing extra appreciation for their local libraries! People visit to check out new books, attend special story times, or simply enjoy the quiet reading spaces. Many libraries host fun events like book swaps, author visits, or reading challenges. Some visitors bring thank-you cards for librarians or donate books to share with others. activity_question: What do some people bring to libraries on Library Lovers Day?

title: Voyager 1's Cosmic Family Portrait
story: Floating billions of miles from home, Voyager 1 turned its camera back toward the sun. The space probe captured a breathtaking view of our entire solar system on February 14, 1990. In this cosmic family portrait, Earth appeared as nothing more than a tiny 'pale blue dot' against the vastness of space. The image, transmitted in real-time, reminded us just how small and precious our planet truly is.

title: Lightning-Fast Feet: Andre Cason's Sprint Record
story: Zoom! American sprinter Andre Cason blazed across the track in Madrid, Spain, setting a new world record. On February 14, 1992, Cason ran the 60-meter indoor sprint in just 6.41 seconds. That's faster than you can say supercalifragilisticexpialidocious! His lightning-quick feet and incredible focus helped him achieve this amazing feat. Cason's record showed what humans can do with dedication and training.

story: Gliding across the ice, Britain's Jayne Torvill and Christopher Dean mesmerized the world in 1984. On February 14, at the Sarajevo Winter Olympics, the duo performed an unforgettable ice dance to Maurice Ravel's 'Bolero'. Their graceful movements and perfect synchronization left judges in awe. title: Home Gold for Canada's Freestyle King

title: Luna 20's Lunar Adventure
story: Blast off! On February 14, 1972, Russia launched Luna 20 on an exciting mission to the Moon. This robotic spacecraft zoomed through space, aiming for our celestial neighbor. Luna 20's goal? To orbit the Moon, then gently touch down on its surface. This 'soft landing' technique allowed the spacecraft to explore the lunar landscape without crashing. It was like a cosmic ballet, carefully choreographed to unlock more secrets of our mysterious Moon.
activity_question: What was Luna 20's mission goal?

To collect space rocks from Mars
To take pictures of Earth from space

On February 14, 1989, this satellite reached orbit, beginning a new era of navigation. Soon, a network of 24 satellites would circle Earth, helping people find their way with pinpoint accuracy.

title: Giant Science Machine Takes a Break

title: Earth's Wobbly Dance Discovered
story: Imagine the Earth doing a slow, wobbly dance! In 1747, astronomer James Bradley made a groundbreaking discovery. On February 14, he presented his findings to the Royal Society in London. Bradley had noticed that our planet doesn't spin perfectly straight on its axis. Instead, it wobbles slightly, like a spinning top that's about to fall. title: Bright Idea Saves Lives

title: Atomic Discovery: Lawrencium Joins the Table

title: A Safe Haven for Feathered Friends
story: Birds got a special gift in 1872! Lake Merritt in California became the first official bird refuge in the United States. On February 14 that year, the government said, 'This place is for the birds!' It meant birds could live there safely without being bothered. Today, many different types of birds still call Lake Merritt home.

story: Hoops fans packed the Pontiac Silverdome in 1987 for an epic showdown. The Detroit Pistons faced off against the Philadelphia 76ers on February 14, drawing a jaw-dropping crowd. story: Whitney Houston burst onto the music scene with her self-titled debut album on February 14, 1985. Her powerful voice and catchy songs quickly captured hearts worldwide. story: Richard Allen made history in 1760. Born on February 14 in Philadelphia, he would grow up to become a trailblazer. Allen became the first African American ordained by the Methodist-Episcopal church. His leadership opened doors for others and helped shape a more inclusive religious community.

story: Charles Wilson, born in Glencorse, Scotland in 1869, would change how we see tiny particles. He invented the Wilson cloud chamber, a device that made invisible particles visible! title: Dancing Queen of the Stage
story: Aida Overton Walker twirled into the world in 1880, born on February 14 in Richmond, Virginia. Walker became a shining star in shows like 'In Dahomey' and wowed crowds with her captivating 'Salome' dance.

story: Charles Oatley, born in From, England in 1904, would change how we see tiny things forever. This electrical engineer had a big idea: make microscopes even more powerful! title: Miss Moneypenny's Real-Life Debut
story: Lights, camera, action! On February 14, 1927, a future spy movie star was born. Lois Maxwell entered the world in Kitchener, Ontario. title: Hockey Legend Bernie Boom Boom Geoffrion
story: Skates sliced the ice as Bernie Geoffrion burst onto the hockey scene. Born in Montreal on February 14, 1931, he earned the nickname Boom Boom for his powerful slapshot. Geoffrion became a star right wing, helping the Montreal Canadiens win six Stanley Cups. His skills led to eleven NHL All-Star selections and a place in the Hockey Hall of Fame.
activity_question: What was Bernie Geoffrion's nickname?

story: Lights, camera, action! Florence Henderson, the beloved TV mom from The Brady Bunch, entered the world in Dale, Indiana in 1934.
